questions.question: In the 6-Step Legacy Action Plan Builder, what does Step 2 emphasize when assessing your current reality?

options.text: Focus on your ideal future life circumstances
Plan from your actual life—time, energy, resources, constraints that REALISTICALLY exist, not the life you wish you had
Compare yourself to others doing similar work
Ignore constraints and dream big without limitations

questions.explanation: Step 2 emphasizes honest inventory of your ACTUAL current reality: time available (not ideal—actual), energy levels and health constraints, financial resources and skills, caregiving and work obligations. The key principle stated is: 'Plan from your actual life, not the life you wish you had. Start where you are.' This ensures your action plan is realistic and sustainable rather than aspirational and likely to fail. Many people create unsustainable plans based on ideal circumstances rather than real constraints—this step prevents that common mistake.

questions.question: Why does the lesson recommend tracking 'process metrics' (like hours spent, sessions held) rather than relying primarily on 'outcome metrics' (like mentee reports change)?

options.text: Outcome metrics don't matter at all
Process metrics are within your control; you can't control outcomes, only inputs. Consistent process predicts eventual outcomes even when outcomes aren't visible yet
Process metrics are easier to fake
Outcome metrics are impossible to measure

questions.explanation: The lesson distinguishes between process metrics (trackable and within your control: hours worked, sessions held, content created, boundaries maintained) and outcome metrics (harder to track, not fully in your control: mentee change, content reach, long-term impact). The key insight: You control inputs, not outcomes. Celebrate outcomes when visible, but don't need these to validate your work. This is especially important for legacy work where impact is often invisible, delayed, and hard to measure. Focusing on consistent process is more sustainable and less discouraging than waiting for visible outcomes that may take years or never be known to you.

questions.question: According to the long-term sustainability timeline, what is the primary focus during Years 4-7 of legacy work?

options.text: Start small and allow mistakes as a beginner
You've found what works—maintain it without burning out. Guard against overexpansion. Focus on financial sustainability and succession planning beginnings
Refine your approach and expand wherever possible
Complete succession planning and retire

questions.explanation: Years 4-7 represent 'Sustainable Impact' phase: You're competent now and have found what works—the focus is maintaining it without burning out while guarding against overexpansion. Key priorities include: financial sustainability model locked in, clear capacity limits (3-4 mentees, defined hours), regular sabbaticals, and succession planning beginnings. The realistic expectation: You're competent but sustainable pacing still requires active boundary-keeping—don't assume you're immune to burnout. Success metric: Longevity without burnout, serving consistently, enjoying the work. This contrasts with Year 1 (foundation building) and Years 8-15 (maturity and full succession planning).

questions.question: The lesson lists 8 common obstacles in legacy work. For 'Obstacle 2: Overcommitment and Boundary Collapse,' what is the recommended mantra?

options.text: 'Good enough to help someone is good enough to start'
'I can serve longer by serving less right now'
'I don't need to be the best. I need to be available'
'Seeds planted today may bloom decades from now'

questions.explanation: For overcommitment and boundary collapse (saying yes to every request, taking too many mentees, working without rest), the mantra is: 'I can serve longer by serving less right now.' The reality check: Unsustainable generosity ends your work entirely. Boundaries serve your mission. The recommended response references Lesson 16 (energy management): implement 3-4 mentee maximum, clear work hours, regular breaks, say no to protect yes to what matters most. This mantra reframes boundaries not as selfishness but as sustainability strategy—you serve MORE people over 20 years by maintaining capacity than by burning out after 2 years.

questions.question: In Step 3 of the Action Plan Builder (Choose 1-2 Specific Starting Points), what criteria are given for selecting these actions?

options.text: As many actions as possible to maximize impact quickly
Whatever others are doing successfully
Specific, time-bounded, aligned with values, realistic given constraints—and ONE OR TWO actions, not ten
The most ambitious goals you can imagine

questions.explanation: Step 3 emphasizes selecting 1-2 concrete actions based on your values (Step 1) and current reality (Step 2). The criteria are: Specific (not vague), time-bounded (with clear timeframe), aligned with values (serves your 'why'), realistic given constraints (accounts for actual time/energy). Critically: ONE or TWO actions, not ten. Examples given include completing life review over 3 months at 1 hour weekly, proposing 6-month mentoring relationship, or creating mini-course outline. The principle: 1-2 specific actions beat 10 vague intentions. This prevents overwhelm and increases likelihood of follow-through.

title: Integration and Your Continuing Legacy Journey - Quiz
